home.hero_desc: Visual mathematics in Hinglish — see it, play it, understand it. For curious minds of any age.
home.show_see_desc: Watch an idea come alive — numbers invented scene by scene, math you actually see move.
home.show_play_desc: Touch the idea within seconds — drag, tap, slide. Understanding through play, not prose.
home.show_aha_desc: The moment it clicks — one clean visual, one line, and the "aha" lands for good.
home.story_desc: Play the invention of numbers, scene by scene — match sheep to stones, slide the digit that becomes 200, drag zero into place. Hands-on, with audio narration.
home.worlds_sub: 8 worlds · basic to beautiful · start anywhere
home.play_sub: Free-play interactive simulations — no lesson structure, just exploration.
home.sim_circle_hook: Why πr²? Slice it like pizza 🍕
home.sim_pyth_hook: a² + b² = c², seen not memorised
home.ages_desc: From a five-year-old counting rotis to a hundred-year-old rediscovering zero — the same path, walked at your own depth. The only prerequisite is curiosity.
home.email_desc: New visual lessons drop weekly. No spam — just math that makes you go "aha."
home.email_note: Unsubscribe any time. No algorithms involved.
home.email_success: ✓ You're in! Watch for the next aha.
home.email_error: Hmm, that didn't go through — please try again.
home.footer_tagline2: Visual mathematics for curious minds of any age.
home.khoj_invite_desc: Today's tap-and-count find. Spot them all, share your score.

khoj.title: Today's Find
khoj.prompt: Tap all the {object} hiding in the scene.
khoj.share_text: I found all {n} {object}! {emoji} Play Aaj ka Khoj → {url}

mango.decoy_bird: That's a bird, not a mango! 🐦

cats.decoy_dog: That's a dog, not a cat! 🐶

error404.title: This page doesn't compute.
error404.body: Some things in maths are undefined — and so is this page. It may have moved, or never existed.

hook.title: Shaadi mein kitni roti chahiye?
hook.body: Imagine you're cooking for 5 guests. You need one roti for each person. How do you know when you have enough? You count — one roti, one guest, one by one. That's all a number is.

play.title: Count the dots
play.instruction: Tap each dot to count it. Watch the number grow — one tap, one count.

aha.title: One finger, one mango — that's a number!
aha.body: A number is just a count. 5 means "five things" — five rotis, five fingers, five mangoes. The symbol '5' is just the name we gave to that count.

check_q1.prompt: Can you count 5 mangoes? Tap each one.

connect.body: You now know what a number actually IS — a count. Next: where does 10 come from? Why does 9 roll over? Meet Zero and Place Value.

circle_area.title: Area of a Circle = πr²
circle_area.subtitle: Why does the formula work? Slice it to see.

hook.title: Pizza slice secret — why πr²?
hook.body: You've used the formula Area = πr². But where does it come from? What if you could CUT a circle into tiny slices, then rearrange them — and watch the formula appear right in front of you?

play.title: Slice and unroll
play.instruction: Watch the circle slice into wedges and unroll into a rectangle. More slices = closer to a perfect rectangle. The formula lives inside this shape!

aha.title: A circle IS a rectangle in disguise!
aha.body: Unrolled: the rectangle's width = half the circumference = πr. Its height = r. Area = πr × r = πr². The formula isn't magic — it's geometry you can see.

check_q1.prompt: The formula πr² has how many parts? Tap each one: π, r, and r.

connect.body: You've unlocked the circle's secret. Next: symmetry shows us why shapes balance. Then on to the algebra world — patterns, unknowns, and Pythagoras!

hook.title: Ancient people had no zero
hook.body: Without zero and place value, you'd need a different symbol for EVERY number. The Hindu mathematicians invented something smarter: put digits in columns. The SAME 10 symbols (0-9) describe every number ever written.

play.title: Watch the tens column fill
play.instruction: Use the slider to count up. When ones reach 9 and you add 1 more — watch what happens to the columns.

aha.title: Ones reset! The carry jumps to tens.
aha.body: 9+1 doesn't create a new digit — it CARRIES. Ones reset to 0, and tens gets 1. The SAME 10 digits (0–9) describe every number ever, because the column they're in multiplies their value.

check_q1.prompt: Set the slider to 14. How many full tens do you see? Tap once for each.

connect.body: Place value is why 2, 22, and 222 look similar but mean completely different things. Next: what if all numbers had a home on a line?

number_line.title: Numbers have an address!
number_line.subtitle: The number line — where every number lives

hook.title: Every number has a home
hook.body: Imagine a road stretching forever in both directions. Zero is at the centre. Every positive number lives to the right, every negative to the left. This road is the number line — and it's the most powerful idea in all of maths.

play.title: Walk the number line
play.instruction: Move the slider and watch the dot travel. Each step is exactly 1 unit. Zero is the origin — the starting point for all distances.

aha.title: Distance = steps from zero
aha.body: 7 is 7 steps right of zero. -4 is 4 steps left. Subtraction is walking backwards. The number line turns all of arithmetic into movement — and that's why it works for everything from temperature to bank balances.

check_q1.prompt: Go to position 5. Tap 5 times to count each step away from zero.

connect.body: The line extends left too — into negative numbers. Temperature, debt, elevation all live there. Next: numbers below zero.

negatives.title: Numbers below zero!
negatives.subtitle: The other half of the number line

hook.title: Your bank account can go below zero
hook.body: You owe a friend ₹50 but have ₹0. After the loan your balance is -₹50. In Shimla in January the temperature is -5°C. These aren't imaginary — they're the LEFT side of the number line, and they've always been there.

play.title: Cross to the other side
play.instruction: Drag the slider left past zero. Watch the dot cross into negative numbers. -3 is exactly 3 steps left of zero — just as real as +3 is to the right.

aha.title: Negatives are mirror images of positives!
aha.body: -5 and +5 are the same distance from zero, just in opposite directions. Add them: 5 + (-5) = 0. They cancel perfectly. Every positive number has a negative twin, and together they make the number line perfectly symmetric.

check_q1.prompt: How far is -4 from zero? Tap 4 times to count each step.

connect.body: Negatives unlock subtraction, temperature scales, debt, and eventually complex numbers. Next world: how do we combine and transform numbers?

hook.title: 3 sweets from Amma + 4 from Nani = ?
hook.body: You don't need to count all 7 from scratch. You already know there are 3. Just count 4 more: 4, 5, 6, 7. Addition is continuing a count from where you left off — and that's all it ever is.

play.title: Watch the groups merge
play.instruction: Watch the two groups of dots combine into one. Try changing the group sizes with the sliders and see how the total changes.

aha.title: Order doesn't matter — the pile is the same!
aha.body: 3 + 4 = 4 + 3 = 7. It's the same pile of dots, just arranged differently. This is the commutative property — and it works for ALL numbers. The pile doesn't care which group came first.

check_q1.prompt: After 3 + 4 merges, tap all 7 dots to count them one by one.

connect.body: Addition is the foundation of everything: multiplication is repeated addition, algebra is finding the missing number in an addition. Next: what is subtraction?

hook.title: A builder's secret from 2500 years ago
hook.body: Ancient builders used a rope with 12 knots tied into a 3-4-5 triangle to make PERFECT right angles. Why does 3² + 4² = 5²? What does it even mean to 'square' a side?

play.title: Count the squares
play.instruction: Watch the squares fill up — square on side a, square on side b, and square on side c. Count the cells. What do you notice?

aha.title: The squares on the legs fit inside the square on the hypotenuse!
aha.body: a² means a square made of a×a cells. b² is b×b cells. Together they exactly fill the c² square. That's not a coincidence — it's true for EVERY right triangle.

check_q1.prompt: In a 3-4-5 triangle: tap 5 times to count the extra cells that c² has over a² alone.

connect.body: Pythagoras works in navigation, architecture, and even your phone's GPS. Next: how do we read patterns from graphs?
